text:
Cyclic structures such as medium-sized (7–11 members) and macrocyclic (12+ members) benzannulated medium-sized and macrocyclic rings (BMRs) fused to an aromatic core are among the most frequently observed in bioactive natural products and drug molecules. Often referred to as the “golden ring”, these structures impose critical strain driven conformations that give rise to improved potency, selectivity and bioavailability, and are therefore commonly employed in the design of anticancer, anti-infective and metabolic diseases therapies. However, their formation is often hampered by strong entropic penalties and transannular repulsions, which restrict their synthesis.

This review offers a general overview of the biological importance of the BMRs and on the robust methods developed to gain access to these sophisticated structures. Standard methodologies as lactonization, lactamization, ring-closing metathesis (RCM) and transition metal-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions, as well as oxidative ring expansion reactions are also described. In addition, carbohydrates have been widely used as chiral building blocks for the synthesis of enantiomerically pure BMR ethers, thio-ethers and lactones. Finally, by describing both the natural and synthetic BMRs, we show the continuous interest of these molecular motifs for drug development and highlight the advances in synthetic methodologies to realize the challenging task of assembling a macrocycle.
